title: Beer on Linen
stainChemistry: Beer contains hop-derived polyphenols (tannins), caramel colorant from malt kilning, and residual sugars (maltose). Fresh beer stains are nearly invisible — pale golden-brown at most. The danger is the 'invisible stain' phenomenon Dan Eisen warns about: sugars caramelize and tannins oxidize with heat, turning invisible beer residue into a dark brown permanent mark during dry cleaning or ironing. Beer's polyphenol concentration is lower than wine, tea, or coffee, but the sugar component adds a caramelization risk that pure tannin stains don't have. On linen (cellulose), both the tannin and sugar hydrogen-bond to cellulose hydroxyl groups.
whyThisWorks: Cold flush removes dissolved sugars and polyphenols before they bond to linen cellulose. The approach treats beer as a combination of low-concentration tannin + sugar. Acetic acid disrupts the polyphenol-cellulose hydrogen bonds. NSD surfactant removes the sugar component that would otherwise caramelize brown with heat exposure. The critical insight from Dan Eisen: 'stains undergo constant chemical change — fruits produce ethylene gas converting starch to sugar.' Beer's sugar oxidizes the same way — treat the sugar component to prevent the delayed browning that makes beer stains notorious. On linen, full-strength treatment is safe — pH 6.0–11.0, all solvents tolerated, all bleach types available.

spottingProtocol.technique: Extended flushing from back of fabric to remove dissolved sugars
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold (60–70°F) — no hot water; sugar caramelizes with heat, tannin oxidizes
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 2–3 minutes — longer flush than wine/coffee because sugar needs dilution
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place linen face-down on white towel. Flush cold water from back — extended flushing because beer's dissolved sugars need volume to dilute and remove. Sugar left in fiber is the time bomb — it's invisible now but turns dark brown when ironed or dry cleaned at machine temperature. Dan Eisen: 'Heat at 140°F sets tannin stains — this is why pre-spotting is required before drycleaning.' Flush until water runs clear through the towel.

spottingProtocol.technique: Surfactant wash to remove residual sugars and caramel colorant
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD solution (2% concentration) or a mild detergent (Tide at Dan Eisen's dilution: ¼ tablespoon per 2 oz water). The surfactant emulsifies and removes sugar residue that cold water alone may not fully dissolve. Tamp gently with brush — linen's wet strength allows moderate mechanical action. Flush with cold water. This step prevents the delayed browning that makes beer the 'stealth stain.'

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go (full strength on linen)
spottingProtocol.technique: Tannin formula for hop polyphenols and caramel colorant
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — steam at safe distance (6+ inches) after tannin formula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Tannin-Go full strength — linen tolerates undiluted acid formula. The acetic acid breaks hydrogen bonds between hop polyphenols and linen cellulose. Even though beer's tannin concentration is lower than wine or tea, the treatment ensures no polyphenol residue remains to oxidize brown later. Dan Eisen includes beer in his tannin stain list alongside 'coffee, tea, wine, soft drinks.' Flush with water gun. One cycle is usually sufficient for beer.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 6% + ammonia catalyst (if visible brown staining)
spottingProtocol.technique: Oxidation for caramelized sugar browning — only if needed
spottingProtocol.temperature: Gentle steam activation safe on linen
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only needed for set/aged beer stains showing brown discoloration (caramelized sugar or oxidized tannin). Flush tannin formula completely. Apply peroxide + ammonia. Steam-activate at 6+ inches — linen handles this well. The HOO⁻ anion breaks down caramelization products and oxidized polyphenol chromophores. Fresh beer stains rarely need this step.

spottingProtocol.technique: Acid rinse to prevent yellowing on linen
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply vinegar solution to neutralize any alkaline residue. Linen is more prone to alkaline yellowing than cotton — this step is non-negotiable if peroxide + ammonia was used. Flush with cold water. Press while damp for best results — linen wrinkles set when dry.

homeSolutions: Fresh spill: Blot immediately and flush generously with cold water — beer's sugars are the hidden enemy. They're invisible now but turn brown with any heat. Flush longer than you think necessary. Follow with mild dish soap in cold water to remove sugar residue.

Set stain (brown caramelization visible): Apply white vinegar directly, let sit 5 minutes, blot. Follow with OxiClean soak in warm water (not hot) for 1–2 hours. Rinse with vinegar (2 oz/gallon) to prevent linen yellowing. Air dry.

When to stop: Beer on linen is one of the easier tannin stains. If brown caramelization persists after vinegar + OxiClean soak, take to a dry cleaner — they'll use professional tannin formula followed by controlled oxidation. Tell them it's beer — the sugar component may have caramelized during an ironing or dry cleaning cycle.
materialWarnings: NEVER iron or press linen before confirming all beer residue is removed — sugar caramelizes at iron temperature, turning invisible residue into a permanent brown mark
NEVER dry clean without wet-side pre-spotting — Dan Eisen: 'heat at 140°F sets tannin stains; pre-spotting is required before drycleaning'
NEVER skip vinegar neutralization after peroxide/ammonia on linen — linen yellows from residual alkali
NEVER use hot water on fresh beer stain — caramelizes sugars and sets tannin simultaneously
Beer stains are 'invisible' when fresh — treat ANY beer spill as if it will turn brown, because it will

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go
professional.use: Full strength on linen — hop polyphenol bond breaker
professional.note: Acetic acid disrupts tannin-cellulose bonds; one cycle usually sufficient for beer's lower tannin load

professional.name: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent)
professional.note: Critical step for beer — removes the sugar component that causes delayed browning

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6%
professional.use: With ammonia catalyst for caramelized sugar browning
professional.note: Only needed for set/aged stains; fresh beer rarely requires oxidation

professional.name: Glacial Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10)
professional.note: Prevents alkaline yellowing that linen is susceptible to

consumer.name: Dawn Dish Soap (Original Blue)
consumer.use: Sugar and residue removal — 1 tsp per cup cold water
consumer.note: First-line defense for fresh beer spills; surfactant removes sugars that vinegar doesn't address

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar
consumer.note: Dual role: acid breaks tannin bonds AND prevents yellowing after any bleach treatment

consumer.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
consumer.use: Soak for set beer with brown caramelization
consumer.note: Warm water soak 1–2 hours; follow with vinegar rinse for linen safety

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.note: Apply after vinegar treatment, wait 3 minutes, rinse. Follow with vinegar. Safe on linen.

escalation.when: Brown caramelization persists after full tannin + oxidizer treatment, suggesting the stain was heat-set during ironing or a prior cleaning cycle
escalation.whatToTell: This is beer on linen — the brown discoloration is likely caramelized sugar from heat exposure (ironing or dry cleaning without pre-spotting). I treated with surfactant (sugar removal), tannin spotter (polyphenols), and oxidizer with vinegar neutralization.
escalation.specialistType: Any professional dry cleaner — linen handles all solvents and aggressive chemistry. All solvents rated excellent. The caramelized sugar may require professional-strength oxidizers or reducing agents that exceed consumer products.

solventNote: CRITICAL: Pre-spot beer stains on wet side before dry cleaning — invisible sugar residue caramelizes at machine temperature (140–150°F) creating a permanent brown mark that wasn't visible when the garment was dropped off. Dan Eisen: 'pre-spotting light colored fabrics before drycleaning prevents stain oxidation.' NSD wash first (sugar), then tannin formula (polyphenols), then level with Sofspot before machine load. Linen handles all solvents — PERC, hydrocarbon, GreenEarth all rated excellent.
